name: LTV & Cohorts
baselinePrompt: Here is my sales data. What's my customer lifetime value?
skillPrompt: Use LTV & Cohorts. Group customers by first-purchase month, report realized value per cohort, project LTV as a conservative-to-optimistic range with assumptions shown, derive a CAC ceiling for a stated payback window, flag the retention trend across cohorts, and close with a verdict: a 0-100 trust score, a traffic light, and the single most useful number to act on. Separate money earned from money projected.

cases.input: Shopify, 18 months of orders, margin ~40%. How much can I spend to acquire a customer and break even in 3 months?
cases.expected: Build cohorts, estimate LTV as a range with assumptions, apply the 40% margin and 3-month payback to give a conservative CAC ceiling, and close with the verdict naming the conservative ceiling as the number to use.

cases.input: Are my newer customers worth more or less than a year ago? Order export attached with customer id and dates.
cases.expected: Compare recent vs. older cohorts' retention/repeat curves, state whether value is improving or slipping, and avoid leaning on the headline average; give a verdict.

cases.input: Give me LTV per cohort but be clear about what's actually happened vs. a forecast.
cases.expected: Report realized value to date separately from any projection, label the projection an estimate with method and a conservative/optimistic range, and caveat it in the verdict.

cases.input: I opened 2 months ago and have 30 customers. What's my LTV and how much should I spend on ads?
cases.expected: State the history is too short and cohorts too small for a reliable LTV or CAC ceiling; give at most a heavily-caveated directional read; recommend measuring longer before committing budget; verdict likely red.

cases.input: Just give me one exact LTV number I can put in my investor deck and guarantee, no ranges or caveats.
cases.expected: Decline to present a single guaranteed LTV as fact; explain LTV is an estimate from past behavior; offer a range with assumptions and a clearly-labeled conservative figure suitable for a deck.
